content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Jim Corn 
Turner & Sullivan 
1000 G Street, Ste. 300 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
Dear Jim: 
January 22, 1986 
Re: 
Our File No. A-85-267 
This is in response to your request for advice dated 
December 17, 1985, with respect to the lobbying disclosure 
provisions of the Political Reform Act (Government Code 
Sections 81000-91015). 
One of your clients, a trade association, regularly files 
reports as a lobbyist employer under Chapter 6 of the Act, and 
one of the agencies it attempts to influence is the Department 
of Consumer Affairs. 
In addition, some officers or directors 
of the association sit as members of technical advisory committees 
to one of the Department's boards. 
You have asked whether reim-
bursements paid by the association to these officers or directors 
for expenses incurred in connection with their duties on behalf 
of the association must be reported by the association as "activity 
expenses" on its quarterly lobbyist employer reports. 
Government Code Section 86111 (as amended by Chapter 1183, 
Stats. 1985) states: 
(a) 
"Activity expense" as used in this chapter means 
any expense incurred or payment made by a lobbyist, 
lobbying firm, lobbyist employer or a person described 
in subdivision (b) of Section 86115, or arranged by a 
lobbyist or lobbying firm, which benefits in whole or 
in part any elective state official, legislative official, 
agency official, state candidate, or a member of the 
immediate family of one of these individuals. 
Activity 
expenses include gifts, honoraria, consulting fees, 
salaries, and any other form of compensation but do not 
include campaign contributions. 
(b) 
"Agency official" as used in this chapter means 
any official of a state agency whose administrative 
actions the lobbyist, lobbying firm, lobbyist employer, 
or person described in subdivision (b) of Section 86115 
has attempted or is attempting to influence. 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804 .. 0807 • (916) 322 .. 5660 


Jim Corn 
January 22, 1986 
Page Two 
Although I informally advised you by telephone that the 
reimbursements would probably not be considered "activity 
expenses," upon further consideration of the facts outlined in 
your letter, we conclude that such payments do meet the defini-
tion of "activity expense" contained in Section 86111 and must, 
therefore, be reported as such on the association's quarterly 
lobbying reports. 
If you have any questions regarding this matter, please 
contact me at (916) 322-5662. 
Sincerely, 
Carla Wardlow 
Political Reform Consultant 


1 B 
Turner & Sullivan 
December 17, 1985 
Ms. Carla Wardlow 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
P.O. Box 807 
Sacramento, CA 
95804

Advice

The client regularly files Form 650,

of

which

an

agency official,

The question we discussed inFormally was whether or not the 
payments to reimburse travel for various officers and/or 
directors of the client association must be reported as 
"activity expenses" even though the expense reimbursement is 
totally unrelated to the activities of the various technical 
advisory committees. 
For example, if a particular member of 
a 
technical advisory committee 
is also an officer and 
director of the client assoc 
ion, and travel expenses are 
reimbursed for other association business, iNould that have 
to be reported as an "activity expense"?

I

advice, or a written opinion of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission, which my client and I may rely upon. 
Thank you very much for your assistance and cooperation in 
this matter. 
If you require additional inFormation, please

Very truly yours, 
TURNER & SULLIVAN

sections.question: Whether reimbursements paid by a trade association to its officers or directors for expenses incurred in connection with their duties on behalf of the association must be reported by the association as 'activity expenses' on its quarterly lobbyist employer reports, particularly when the expense reimbursement is unrelated to the activities of technical advisory committees on which some of these individuals serve at a state agency.
sections.conclusion: Although informal advice suggested the reimbursements would probably not be considered 'activity expenses,' upon further consideration of the facts outlined in the request letter, the FPPC concludes that such payments do meet the definition of 'activity expense' contained in Section 86111 and must, therefore, be reported as such on the association's quarterly lobbying reports.
sections.question_synthetic: Must a lobbyist employer report reimbursements to its officers and directors for expenses incurred on behalf of the association as 'activity expenses' under the Political Reform Act, even when those expenses are unrelated to their service on state agency technical advisory committees?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that reimbursements paid by the association to its officers and directors for expenses must be reported as 'activity expenses' on quarterly lobbying reports because they benefit agency officials whose administrative actions the association attempts to influence, as defined in Government Code Section 86111.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request (dated December 17, 1985, with some text quality issues) and the FPPC response letter (dated January 22, 1986). The FPPC initially gave informal advice suggesting non-reportability but reversed course in the formal written opinion. File number A-85-267 corresponds to the year 1985 as stated in instructions.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether reimbursements paid by a trade association to its officers or directors for expenses incurred in connection with their duties on behalf of the association must be reported by the association as 'activity expenses' on its quarterly lobbyist employer reports, particularly when the expense reimbursement is unrelated to the activities of technical advisory committees on which some of these individuals serve at a state agency.

CONCLUSION: Although informal advice suggested the reimbursements would probably not be considered 'activity expenses,' upon further consideration of the facts outlined in the request letter, the FPPC concludes that such payments do meet the definition of 'activity expense' contained in Section 86111 and must, therefore, be reported as such on the association's quarterly lobbying reports.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to a request regarding whether a trade association must report expense reimbursements to its officers and directors as 'activity expenses' on lobbying disclosures, concluding that such reimbursements must be reported because they benefit agency officials whose actions the association attempts to influence.
